gsm_s01.jpg: A professional academic timeline infographic illustrating the evolution of mobile cellular generations from 1G to 4G. The horizontal timeline spans from the early 1980s to the 2010s, with labeled milestones for each generation: '1G Analog (NMT/AMPS)', '2G GSM (Digital)', '3G UMTS', and '4G LTE (All-IP)'. For each milestone, a small icon represents the corresponding technology: an analog radio transceiver for 1G, a classic Nokia 3310 for 2G, a early smartphone for 3G, and a modern LTE modem for 4G. Key technological breakthroughs like 'SMS', 'Internet', 'HD Video', and 'VoLTE' are annotated along the timeline. The visual style is strictly academic and professional, using a clean timeline design with a dark blue and silver color palette. No abstract or decorative elements are present. The background is a minimalist neutral white for maximum clarity. All text is in English, and the composition is balanced, serving as a definitive introductory overview for a mobile network engineering lecture.
gsm_s02.jpg: A professional and historically accurate technical illustration of a 1G NMT (Nordic Mobile Telephone) car phone system. The central focus is a large, car-mounted telephone handset in a 1980s vehicle interior, connected to a bulky transceiver unit installed in the trunk. Callout lines identify the 'FM Radio Transceiver Unit', 'Control Head / Handset', and 'Roof-mounted Antenna'. A side comparison visual shows the 450 MHz analog spectrum allocation, labeled as 'FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)'. A note specifies 'No Encryption' and 'Analog FM Modulation' as key technical limitations. The style is strictly academic and realistic, using a professional illustration style with clean technical line-art. No abstract or non-technical elements are included. The color palette is composed of professional grays, blues, and period-appropriate earth tones for the hardware. All text is in English, providing a technically accurate historical reference for a telecommunications engineering lecture.
gsm_s03.jpg: A professional and academic illustration celebrating the GSM (Global System for Mobile) standard as a global cellular revolution. The image features a large world map with GSM coverage areas highlighted in a professional blue. Iconic classic mobile phones from the early-to-mid 1990s era, such as a Nokia 2110 or Motorola StarTAC, are represented as professional hardware renders. Technical highlights list 'Digital Transmission (TDMA/FDMA)', 'SIM Card Authentication', and 'International Roaming' as key innovations. A small diagram shows the SMS message pathway, labeled as a landmark feature. Logos of early European GSM operators are subtly displayed to show the global adoption. The style is strictly technical and academic, using clean vector graphics and professional typography. No abstract or decorative elements are used. The background is a clean, neutral white for maximum clarity. All text is in English, providing a definitive overview of 2G's impact on mobile communications.
gsm_s04.jpg: A detailed technical block diagram of the overall GSM network architecture, divided into three main subsystems. The 'BSS (Base Station Subsystem)' block contains a BTS and BSC icon. The 'NSS (Network and Switching Subsystem)' block contains labeled nodes for MSC, HLR, and VLR. The 'OSS (Operation Support Subsystem)' block shows a monitoring workstation. Logical interface lines connect all three subsystems, labeled with their standard GSM interface names (Um, A-bis, A). A callout box explains the analogy: BSS is the 'hands', NSS is the 'brain', and OSS is the 'management'. The style is strictly academic and textbook-like, using clean 2D geometric blocks and professional typography. No abstract or decorative elements are included. The background is a neutral white, and the color scheme uses professional tech blues, silver, and white. All labeling is in English, providing an expert-level architectural overview for a mobile networking course.
gsm_s05.jpg: A technical architecture diagram showing the functional hierarchy of the GSM BSS subsystem. The diagram illustrates the signal path from a 'Mobile Station (MS)' (phone icon) to a 'BTS (Base Transceiver Station)' (tower icon) to a 'BSC (Base Station Controller)' (server icon). Interface labels are clearly marked: 'Um (Air Interface)' between MS and BTS, and 'A-bis' between BTS and BSC. A callout highlights that one BSC can control '50-100 BTS units'. The BSC block is labeled as containing the 'Handover Decision Logic' and 'Radio Resource Management'. The style is strictly academic and professional, using clean vector graphics and clear technical labeling in English. No decorative or abstract elements are present. The background is a neutral white, and the color scheme uses professional tech blues and whites. This illustration serves as a definitive guide for understanding the GSM radio access network hierarchy.
